cards.title: What Microservices Actually Are
cards.teaser: A microservices architecture has dozens or hundreds of small services, each owning a single capability, each independently deployable, each with its own data store. The "small" and "independent" are both load-bearing.

runs.text: Microservices are easy to caricature and harder to define rigorously. The defining properties:

items.text: — each service owns a narrow capability. "Authenticate users" or "calculate shipping cost," not "manage everything about orders."
— each ships on its own pipeline; one service's deploy doesn't affect another.
— each service has its own database (or schema). No cross-service queries.
— synchronous (REST, gRPC) or async (events). Never shared state.
— usually a single team can hold one service in its head and ship without coordination.
— each service represents a coherent piece of the domain.

runs.text: Microservices are
not

items.text: A service-based architecture has small services too. The difference is granularity and operational independence.
Many teams shouldn't split their monolith. The right move is often a modular monolith.
They scale, but so do most other architectures with proper engineering.

runs.text: The 2014-2019 hype made "microservices" synonymous with "modern architecture." The 2020s have been a long correction. Many teams have moved back to service-based or modular monoliths after discovering the operational cost.

The same lesson as picking the right number of houses. A house per family member sounds independent and clean. The maintenance bill, the coordination, the shared infrastructure — they punish you. Most families want one house with a few rooms, not many houses.

cards.title: SOA — The Predecessor
cards.teaser: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) was the 2000s pattern that microservices descended from. Coarse services, central orchestration, often a heavy enterprise service bus. SOA is mostly historical now; microservices took its lessons and shrunk the services.

runs.text: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) was the architectural pattern of the 2000s enterprise:

items.text: — broader than microservices; closer to "service-based" in modern terms.
— a heavy middleware layer that routed and transformed messages between services.
— SOAP, WSDL, BPEL, XML. Lots of XML.
— architects defined services centrally; teams implemented to spec.
— one "Customer service" used by every application.

runs.text: What worked: the idea of services as units of business capability, with clear contracts and reusability.
What didn't:

items.text: Heavy middleware that every team depended on. Changes were slow; failures were catastrophic.
Reusability across the enterprise was overpromised.
Services designed for many consumers became generic, brittle, and slow to evolve.
The architecture team became a constraint on velocity.
Enterprise stacks were expensive, complex, and rarely loved by developers.

runs.text: Microservices took the "services with clear capabilities" idea, ditched the ESB and the centralised governance, and scaled the granularity down. The result is more flexible, more decoupled, and easier for individual teams to own.

SOA still exists in plenty of large enterprises that adopted it 15-20 years ago and haven't migrated. The replacement isn't usually "microservices everywhere"; it's "modular services with lightweight integration."

Same as the difference between mainframes and PCs. Mainframes had one big shared resource; PCs distributed compute. Microservices to SOA is the same shape — distribute the work; ditch the central bus.

cards.title: When Microservices Earn Their Keep
cards.teaser: Many teams shipping concurrently. Massive scale. Heterogeneous tech needs. Strong fault isolation requirements. When at least three of these are real, microservices start paying off.

runs.text: The honest set of conditions where microservices are the right choice:

items.text: 50+ engineers, organised into 5+ teams. Each team owns its services and ships independently.
Different parts of the system have wildly different scaling needs.
Different services genuinely benefit from different stacks (Python for data, Go for high-throughput APIs, Rust for systems-level work).
A failure in one service must not cascade. Critical for high-availability systems.
The team can run distributed systems — observability, deployment, on-call, incident response.
Services need to live close to users in different regions, with different compliance requirements.

runs.text: When most of these are absent, microservices are operational overhead with little benefit. The team pays for the architecture; the architecture doesn't pay back.

The most common reason teams pick microservices and regret it: the team isn't large enough. With 10 engineers, every microservice is owned by 1-2 people; vacation, illness, or attrition causes ownership gaps. The coordination costs exceed the team independence benefits because there's no team independence to gain.

"We'll start with microservices because we'll grow into them" is the most expensive shortcut in software architecture. You'll spend two years operating a system that doesn't fit your team — by the time you've grown, the architecture is calcified and migration is multi-quarter work.

The right number of teams depends on the work. A startup of 10 doesn't need 7 departments; that's overhead. A company of 1000 needs them; that's organisation. Microservices are department-shaped; you need to be a company-shaped team to use them.

cards.title: The Operational Tax
cards.teaser: Microservices' biggest cost is operational. Many deploys, many log streams, many monitoring dashboards, many on-call responsibilities. The platform team that makes it tractable is itself a major investment.

runs.text: The operational footprint of microservices, compared to a single monolith:

items.text: Aggregated centrally; distributed tracing required to follow a request.
Or one rotation that covers all of it.
N container images, N registries, N image lifecycles.
— Istio, Linkerd, Cilium. Adds capability and operational surface.
— Datadog, Honeycomb, Grafana stack. Requires investment.
Each service authenticates each other; service identity is its own subsystem.
A dedicated group that maintains the shared infrastructure. Often 3-10 engineers' worth.

runs.text: For a small team, this is a lot. For a large team, it's the price of entry.

The architecture works when the platform team's cost is less than the engineering velocity gain across all the product teams it serves. Below a certain organisation size, the math doesn't work — you're paying platform costs without enough product teams to amortise them.

The "platform engineering" discipline that matured in 2020-2026 is largely a response to this. Companies that succeed at microservices have invested in platform — internal developer platforms, golden paths, paved roads. Without it, every team rebuilds the wheel.
The same lesson as scaling any organisation. Adding a department adds capacity
and
HR overhead. Below a certain scale, the HR overhead exceeds the capacity benefit. Microservices are no different — the architecture has overhead that scales with team count.

cards.title: Bounded Contexts and Service Granularity
cards.teaser: A microservice should map to one bounded context. Too coarse and it's a service-based service; too fine and you're in distributed-monolith territory. The granularity question is where most architectural mistakes live.

runs.text: The right size for a microservice is a topic of long debate. Some heuristics that help:

items.text: A capability with its own vocabulary, its own data, its own team. "Identity," "billing," "pricing," "inventory."
If two teams have to coordinate on every change, the service is in the wrong place — or the team boundary is.
The service should have its own scaling profile. If it doesn't, why is it a separate service?
Most requests should be served without calling out to many other services.

runs.text: Common granularity mistakes:

items.text: Too fine. The CRUD microservices that just wrap a table aren't services; they're remote data-access objects. Pulling them together into a coarser service usually wins.
UI changes too often; services should be more durable.
Microservice per developer or team without thought.
Microservice that owns multiple business capabilities.
"Order service" that does pricing, fulfilment, and notifications is too coarse — it's three services in a trench coat.

runs.text: When in doubt, start coarser and split. Going from "this is too coarse" to splitting is mechanical. Going from "this is too fine" to consolidating is multi-service refactoring. The reverse direction is much harder.
Same as picking a team size. Too big and coordination dominates; too small and you can't get anything done. The right size is the smallest that can independently own meaningful work.

cards.title: Distributed Data — The Hard Part
cards.teaser: Microservices' database-per-service rule is non-negotiable. Cross-service queries, distributed transactions, eventual consistency — all the costs you don't have in a monolith become daily concerns.

runs.text: The hardest part of microservices isn't the services — it's the data.
What you give up:

items.text: A "place order" operation that touches Inventory, Payment, and Order must use sagas, not transactions. Each step has compensating logic.
Reports that need data from five services can't run a single SQL JOIN. They aggregate via API calls (slow) or via dedicated reporting services with replicated data (complex).
The user_id in the Order service is just a value; nothing enforces it points to a real user.
Eventual consistency becomes the norm; UI patterns and business logic must accept it.

runs.text: What you build instead:

items.text: Services publish events; reporting services consume and build their own views.
Retries are normal; double-processing must be safe.
When something goes wrong, you need to follow it across services.
Cross-service schemas are public; backward compatibility matters.

runs.text: Teams that retain a shared database while calling themselves microservices are running a
distributed monolith

— distributed-systems pain with monolith coupling. This is the worst combination and unfortunately common. The database-per-service rule is the line that separates microservices from this anti-pattern.

The same shift as moving from a single bank account to many. A bank account makes "move money from savings to checking" easy. Many accounts in many banks make every transfer a coordinated process. Microservices have the same data shape; the work is much harder.

cards.title: Service Mesh and Communication Infrastructure
cards.teaser: At enough services, you stop thinking about HTTP calls and start thinking about traffic. Service mesh — Istio, Linkerd, Cilium — provides the abstraction. Adds capability and ongoing operational work.

runs.text: A service mesh is the network-layer infrastructure that handles cross-service concerns:

items.text: — service A finds service B without hard-coded addresses.
— requests spread across instances of B.
— services authenticate each other; traffic is encrypted in flight.
— automatic metrics and traces for every cross-service call.
— when B is failing, calls to it fail fast instead of hanging.
— canary deploys, A/B testing, header-based routing.

runs.text: Without a mesh, each service has to implement these itself (or via libraries — the older "fat client" approach). With a mesh, the network handles them; services just make calls.
The mesh isn't free:

items.text: Each call adds proxy hops; latency increases by milliseconds.
The mesh itself has to be operated, monitored, and upgraded.
New abstractions for the team to understand.

runs.text: For systems with 10+ services and real cross-service traffic, the mesh usually pays back. For smaller systems, the overhead exceeds the benefit. Most large microservices deployments in 2026 use Istio, Linkerd, or eBPF-based meshes (Cilium); smaller deployments often skip the mesh entirely.
The mesh is what makes microservices operationally tractable at scale. If your team is going to grow into 30+ services, plan for a mesh. If you're staying smaller, you may not need one.
The traffic system in a city. A village doesn't need traffic lights; they're overhead. A city needs them; without, gridlock. The mesh is traffic infrastructure for the city of services.

cards.title: API Gateways and Backends-for-Frontends
cards.teaser: External clients talk to a gateway, not to individual services. The gateway aggregates, transforms, and authenticates. BFF (backends-for-frontends) extends the pattern — one gateway per client type.

runs.text: External traffic to a microservices system goes through an
API gateway

items.text: — clients talk to one address; the gateway routes internally.
— one client request can fan out to multiple services; the gateway merges responses.
— checked once at the gateway; passed through as identity.
— per client, per endpoint, per region.
— services emit canonical formats; the gateway shapes them for clients.

runs.text: A common refinement:
backends-for-frontends (BFF)
. Instead of one gateway for all clients, have one gateway per client type:

items.text: — serves the web app, with web-shaped responses.
— serves the mobile app, with mobile-optimised payloads (smaller, fewer fields).
— serves third-party API consumers, with stable, versioned contracts.

runs.text: Each BFF aggregates from internal services; each is owned by the client team that uses it. The pattern decouples internal service evolution from client expectations — internal services can change as long as the BFF maintains its contract.
BFFs are particularly useful when mobile constraints differ from web. Mobile apps want fewer round-trips; the BFF aggregates and pre-shapes. Web BFFs can be more permissive about chattiness.

The same instinct as a hotel concierge. The guest doesn't talk to the kitchen, the laundry, the spa, and the front desk separately. The concierge does the routing. The gateway is the system's concierge.

cards.title: When Microservices Fail
cards.teaser: The most common failure: too many services, too few engineers, no platform investment. The system is technically microservices and operationally a swamp. Recovery is consolidation, not more abstraction.

runs.text: The recurring failure modes:

items.text: A team of 10 has 30 services; each owned by 1 person; nobody can take a vacation.
Services share a database; "microservices" is the marketing layer over what's actually a tightly-coupled system.
New services spawn without thought; no inventory; no clear ownership.
The team can't deploy, monitor, or debug at the scale they've reached.
Most features touch many services; coordinating ships is the new bottleneck.

runs.text: The fix when these patterns emerge isn't usually "more microservices best practices." It's consolidation:

items.text: If service A and B always deploy as a pair, they should be one service.
Instead of two services accessing the same data, give one service ownership and the other a clean API.
Some "services" are really library functions someone exposed over HTTP. Bring them back into the calling service.
If you can't get out of microservices, invest in the platform team that makes them tractable.

runs.text: In 2024-2026, "we're consolidating our microservices into a modular monolith" became a routine engineering blog post. The retreat is the right move when the architecture isn't paying off.
Same as any over-built system. The right answer when the bridge is too complex isn't more engineers — it's a simpler bridge. Microservices that aren't paying back deserve the same response.

cards.title: Microservices in 2026 — A Mature View
cards.teaser: Microservices are a tool, not a virtue. The architecture is right for some teams at some scales. The discourse has matured past "microservices everywhere" into "microservices when warranted." That's the right place to land.

runs.text: The honest 2026 view of microservices:

items.text: Modular monolith is. Microservices are an upgrade for teams that have outgrown it.
They earn their keep at scale.
Past 50+ engineers, with real team independence pressure and operational maturity, microservices outperform alternatives.
Platform engineering, operational tooling, distributed-systems expertise. Budget for it.
API discipline, schema versioning, backward compatibility. Without these, microservices are chaos.
Paved roads, golden paths, internal developer platforms. The team velocity comes from the platform; without it, every team reinvents.
A typical large company has microservices for the core platform, modular monoliths for newer products, layered architectures for back-office tools. Mix is fine.

runs.text: The teams that succeed with microservices in 2026 are the ones that grew into the architecture rather than picked it as a starting point. The ones that started with microservices and survived did so because they invested heavily in platform from day one.

The "monolith first" advice from Martin Fowler in 2015 is still the right starting point for almost every team. A decade of evidence has not changed the calculus — most teams that ignored it regretted it.

The right tool for the right job, at the right scale. A microservices architecture is the metropolitan transit system — invaluable in cities of millions, completely unnecessary in towns of thousands. Pick based on your population.
